coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots in situ coupling to bismuth oxyiodide via reactable ionic liquid with enhanced photocatalytic molecular oxygen activation performance
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Di, Jun
Xia, Jiexiang
Ji, Mengxia
Xu, Li
Yin, Sheng
Zhang, Qi
Chen, Zhigang
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/bismuth oxyiodide (BiOI) materials have been synthesized via a reactable ionic liquid (IL) 1-hexyl-3-methyl-imidazolium iodine assisted solvothermal method. The introduction of IL was beneficial to construct the tight junctions between CQDs and BiOI. Based on the initial formation of strong-coupling between CQDs and IL, the tight junctions between CQDs and BiOI can be constructed as the iodide ions in the IL react with bismuth nitrate to in situ form BiOI during solvothermal treatment. The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared CQDs/BiOI materials was evaluated using rhodamine B (RhB) as target pollutant. The CQDs/BiOI materials displayed the enhanced photocatalytic performance than pure BiOI while the 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI materials showed the highest activity. The enhanced photocatalytic activity can be ascribed to the cooperation effect of the excellent contact interface between BiOI and CQDs, while the effective separation of charge carriers via CQDs and the CQDs acted as photocatalytic reaction centers. The main active species during the photodegradation process was determined and photocatalytic mechanism was proposed based on the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy valence band spectra, active species trapping experiments and electron spin resonance analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for CQDs and CQDs/BiOI materials with different CQDs contents. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
FT-IR spectra of CQDs/BiOI materials with different CQDs contents. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS spectra of BiOI and 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI samples: (a) survey of the sample (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s, (d) I 3d and (e) C 1s. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SEM images of (a) pure BiOI and (b–d) 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI sample; TEM images of 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI materials (e) Low magnification TEM image; (f) high magnification TEM image. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the as-prepared samples and (b) (αEphoton)1/2 vs. Ephoton curves of the as-prepared BiOI materials. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Nitrogen absorption–desorption isotherms of (a) pure BiOI and (b) 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI materials. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the presence of pure BiOI and CQDs/BiOI samples under visible light irradiation; (b) Kinetic fit for the degradation of RhB with the pure BiOI and CQDs/BiOI samples. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Transient photocurrent response of pure BiOI and CQDs/BiOI materials under visible light irradiation; (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of pure BiOI and CQDs/BiOI materials. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS valence band spectra of the pure BiOI and 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI sample. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra recorded with (a, b) pure BiOI and (c, d) 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI samples in (a, c) methanol dispersion (for DMPO-O2
−) and (b, d) aqueous dispersion (for DMPO-·OH) under visible light irradiation. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Comparison of photocatalytic activities of 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI materials for the degradation of RhB with or without adding IPA, BQ and AO under visible light irradiation. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic of the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges in the CQDs/BiOI combined with the possible reaction mechanism of photocatalytic procedure. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots in situ coupling to bismuth oxyiodide via reactable ionic liquid with enhanced photocatalytic molecular oxygen activation performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hainan Provincial Key Lab of Fine Chemistry, Hainan University, Haikou, 570228, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hainan Provincial Key Lab of Fine Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/bismuth oxyiodide (BiOI) materials have been synthesized via a reactable ionic liquid (IL) 1-hexyl-3-methyl-imidazolium iodine assisted solvothermal method. The introduction of IL was beneficial to construct the tight junctions between CQDs and BiOI. Based on the initial formation of strong-coupling between CQDs and IL, the tight junctions between CQDs and BiOI can be constructed as the iodide ions in the IL react with bismuth nitrate to in situ form BiOI during solvothermal treatment. The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared CQDs/BiOI materials was evaluated using rhodamine B (RhB) as target pollutant. The CQDs/BiOI materials displayed the enhanced photocatalytic performance than pure BiOI while the 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI materials showed the highest activity. The enhanced photocatalytic activity can be ascribed to the cooperation effect of the excellent contact interface between BiOI and CQDs, while the effective separation of charge carriers via CQDs and the CQDs acted as photocatalytic reaction centers. The main active species during the photodegradation process was determined and photocatalytic mechanism was proposed based on the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy valence band spectra, active species trapping experiments and electron spin resonance analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: The semiconductor photocatalysis using solar energy has been regarded as one of the most effective strategies to produce hydrogen energy and solve water pollution problems by degrading pollutants [1,2]. TiO2 which has been consider as one of the most widely investigated photocatalysts for its superior photocatalytic activity, excellent chemical stability and low cost [3]. However, the TiO2 could only be sensitive to UV light, which greatly limited its extensive applications [4]. From the viewpoint of efficient utilization of solar energy, the exploitation of visible-light-active photocatalysts is essential for practical applications. Numerous researchers have committed to develop visible-light-driven photocatalysts, and a large number of visible light active photocatalysts have been synthesized [5–8].

Recently, bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, X = Cl, Br, and I) has attracted greatly attention due to its superior photocatalytic activity under UV or visible light irradiation [9–18]. It belongs to the family of main group multicomponent metal oxyhalides V-VI-VII, which is built by interlacing [Bi2O2] slabs with double halogen slabs to form a layered structure [19]. Owing to strong intralayer bonding and weak interlayer van der Waals interactions, the self-built internal static electric fields can be produced and accelerated the separation of the photo-generated charge carries, which resulting the superior photocatalytic activity [20]. Among these compounds, bismuth oxyiodide (BiOI) have received widely attentions for its strongest absorption in the visible light range [21–24]. However, the photocatalytic performance of BiOI was still far from enough for potential industrial applications and it is necessary to apply strategies for optimization. Up to now, various strategies have been employed to further improve the photocatalytic performance, such as nanoscaling [25], tailoring crystal facets [26], doping [27], surface modification [28,29], and coupling with other photocatalysts [30–35]. However, the contact interfaces of these hybrid systems were insufficient and incompact as well as surface defects of the interfaces would appear, which could act as recombination center of photo-generated charge carriers and limit the photocatalytic performance. Therefore, it would be desirable if small size materials could be introduced to the BiOI materials to construct high disperse and tight touch interfaces.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with the size below 10 nm, are fascinating carbon materials which have attracted massive research interests as they possess many benefits, such as chemical inertness, low cytotoxicity, low photobleaching, and excellent biocompatibility [36,37]. The CQDs are versatile and can be employed in wide fields, such as bioimaging [38], lasers [39], sensing [40], and energy conversion/storage devices [41]. Very recently, CQDs have been applied in photocatalysis field due to their excellent electron transfer ability. Some CQDs/semiconductor systems, such as CQDs/TiO2 [42–45], CQDs/Bi2WO6 [46], CQDs/Fe3O4 [47], CQDs/C3N4 [48], CQDs/Cu2O [49], and CQDs/Ag3PO4 [50], have been prepared and improved photocatalytic activity has been obtained. However, the structure–activity relationships need to be investigated in details and the photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs-based photocatalysts still need to be further explored. In addition, the present studies about the CQDs-based composites were still relatively insufficient while seldom researchers have paid their attentions on the enhanced coupling interaction between the semiconductor and CQDs. Considering the importance of charge carriers transfer between the semiconductor and CQDs for the improved photocatalytic activity, it is significant and essential to control their interface interaction so as to prepare highly efficient CQDs-semiconductor photocatalysts.

Recent researches have found that the ionic liquid (IL) could act as solvent, template, reactant, dispersing agent, capping agent and so on, which plays vital role on the formation of various inorganic functional materials [51,52]. Thus, the IL was introduced to assist prepare CQDs modified BiOI materials in this system. The hydrogen bond and coulomb force are important noncovalent interaction in the IL system [53], which could act on OH or carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs. The combination could act as the nucleation sites for the reaction and facilitated the strong bonding between the BiOI and CQDs [54].

In this system, the CQDs/BiOI microsphere materials were prepared via a solvothermal method in the presence of reactable IL 1-hexyl-3-methyl-imidazolium iodine ([Hmim]I). The introduction of IL favored the construction of the tight junctions between CQDs and BiOI. The formation mechanism of the strong-coupling CQDs/BiOI materials based on the initial formation of strong-coupling CQDs-IL and subsequent the iodide ions in the IL react with bismuth nitrate to in situ form BiOI during solvothermal treatment. The sufficient and tight contact interface between BiOI and CQDs could favor the separation of photogenerated charge carries. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BiOI materials were evaluated by the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. The key roles of CQDs for the improved photocatalytic performance of CQDs/BiOI materials were systematically characterized and discussed. The photocatalytic mechanism was proposed based on the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy valence band spectra, active species trapping experiments and electron spin resonance analysis.

All the reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. The IL [Hmim]I (99%) was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co. Ltd. The CQDs solid was prepared according to the literature followed by the freeze-drying process [55].

For the synthesis of CQDs/BiOI materials, 1 mmol [Hmim]I and a certain amount of CQDs were dispersed into 8 mL ethylene glycol (EG) to obtain solution A. 1 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved into 8 mL distilled water together with 1 mL acetic acid to obtain solution B. The solution A was added into solution B dropwise under stirring condition and then the mixing solution was stirred for another 30 min. Finally, the obtained solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and heated to 140 °C for 24 h. After the solvothermal reaction, the as-prepared products were acquired by centrifuge and rinsed adequately with ethanol and distilled water. The samples were dried in an oven at 50 °C for 12 h before further characterization and using in photocatalytic reactions. The obtained composite was denoted as X wt% CQDs/BiOI, where X wt% was the initial weight ratio of CQDs. For comparison, pure BiOI was prepared with the same method.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer) with Cu Kα radiation was employed to characterize the crystal structure of the as-prepared materials. Fourier Transform Infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained using a Nexus 470 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet, America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a VG MultiLab 2000 system with a monochromatic Mg Kα source. The morphology of the sample was characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JSM-7001F) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) operated at an acceleration voltage of 10 kV. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were measured by a JEOL JEM-2010 instrument at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The UV–vis DRS of the samples were recorded with a Shimadzu UV2450 spectrophotometer. Surface areas of the as-prepared materials were measured using Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method performed on a TriStar II 3020 Surface Area and Porosity Analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA). The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of spin-trapped oxidative radicals were acquired on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer equipped with a quanta-Ray Nd:YAG laser system as the light source. The photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were measured on an electrochemical system (CHI-660B). A standard three-electrode cell with CQDs/BiOI/ITO as a working electrode, a platinum wire as a counter electrode, and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode were used in photoelectric measurement. The photocurrent and EIS were carried out in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.0), and 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4−, respectively. A 500 W Xe arc lamp was used as light source.

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared CQDs/BiOI materials was evaluated by the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) in solution under visible light irradiation. The visible light source was obtained by a 300 W Xe lamp with a 400 nm cutoff filter. 30 mg of sample was dispersed into 100 mL RhB aqueous solution (10 mg L−1). Before the irradiation, the suspensions were magnetically stirred for 30 min to reach the absorption–desorption equilibrium. At given time intervals, 3 mL suspensions were sampled and centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst particles. The filtrates of RhB solutions were analyzed using an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu) according to its absorbance at 553 nm. To investigate the active species generated during the photocatalysis process, the experiments of active species (OH, O2
− and h+) trapping were performed by adding 1 mmol isopropyl alcohol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ) and ammonium oxalate (AO), respectively.

The crystal structures and phase data for the as-prepared samples were determined by XRD analysis. As shown in Fig. 1 , all the diffraction peaks of the as-prepared BiOI sample can be ascribed to bismuth oxyiodide with lattice constants of a = 3.994 Å, c = 9.149 Å, which matched well with the standard XRD patterns (JCPDS No. 10-0445). The XRD patterns of the CQDs exhibited a broad peak centered at 26°, which was attributed to the highly disordered carbon atoms [55,56]. With respect to CQDs/BiOI materials, all the diffraction peaks were well-matched to the BiOI. However, no diffraction peak of CQDs could be observed when compared with the pure BiOI sample, which was attributed to its low amount in the CQDs/BiOI materials [46,50].

The presence of CQDs in CQDs/BiOI materials was determined by FT-IR analysis, and the result was shown in Fig. 2 . It can be found that the absorption band of pure BiOI at 485 cm−1 was attributed to the Bi–O stretching mode [22]. With respect to CQDs/BiOI materials, the surface functional groups of the CQDs were detected, the peaks at 1698 and 1653 cm−1 were arisen from the CO stretching [56], 1436 cm−1 belonged to the symmetric carboxylate stretch [57], 1532 cm−1 was ascribed to bending vibrations of N–H [55], which indicating the existence of CQDs. The FT-IR spectra do not show any clear evidence for bands assignable to imidazolium, which can be assumed that the cation of ionic liquids can be easily removed from the surface of the as-prepared materials by washing with ethanol and distilled water.

XPS analysis was performed on pure BiOI and typical 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI materials to determine the surface chemical composition. The full survey spectrum (Fig. 3 a) showed that the CQDs/BiOI materials sample was composed of Bi, O, I and C elements. The XPS spectrum of samples in the Bi 4f region showed binding energies at 158.8 eV for Bi 4f7/2 and 164.0 eV for Bi 4f5/2, respectively, suggesting that Bi existed in the chemical states of Bi3+ (Fig. 3b) [34]. The O 1s peak at binding energies of 529.7 eV (Fig. 3c) belonged to the oxygen in BiOI materials. The two strong peaks at 618.7 and 630.2 eV corresponded to the binding energies of I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 4 c) [35]. From the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, the signals at 284.7, 286.5, and 287.9 eV demonstrated the presence of CC, oxygenated carbon and nitrous carbon functional groups, respectively [55]. The result of XPS analysis was in accordance with FT-IR spectroscopy, which further confirmed the coexistence of BiOI and CQDs in the CQDs/BiOI materials.

The morphological information of the as-synthesized CQDs/BiOI materials photocatalyst was detected by the field emission SEM and the TEM. The SEM image of pure BiOI materials was shown in Fig. 4a, and the disordered aggregation of nanosheets can be found. After the CQDs were introduced, the sphere-like structure with an average diameter of 4.5 μm can be seen (Fig. 4b, c), which indicated that the CQDs could induce the nanosheets' assemble. This was attributed to the abundant surface functional groups of the CQDs, which could act as the nucleation sites for the reaction. As can be seen from the high-magnification SEM image (Fig. 4d), there were nanosheets distribute on the surface of the sphere-like structure and the thickness of nanosheets was about 20 nm. The superficial porous structure which resulted from the accumulation of nanosheets can be found. The as-prepared CQDs/BiOI material was further investigated by TEM characterization. The TEM image shown in Fig. 4e further indicated that the as-prepared CQDs/BiOI material was microsphere structure with diameter of 3.5 μm. Fig. 4f showed the arrangement of the CQDs and BiOI via HRTEM. From the HRTEM image, the interplanar distances of BiOI crystallites were determined to be 0.229 and 0.301 nm, corresponding to the (004) and (102) crystallographic planes, respectively. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.321 nm was in accordance with the (002) crystal plane of CQDs [46,50]. The (004) crystal plane of BiOI can be found to have tight contact with CQDs. This result strongly demonstrated that the CQDs and BiOI crystallites have constructed excellent combination.

The optical absorption properties of the as-prepared BiOI and CQDs/BiOI samples were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectra and the results were displayed in Fig. 5 a. The typical DRS of all samples showed intense absorption bands with a steep edge in the visible light region. The steep absorption edge revealed that the visible-light absorption was attributed to the band gap transition rather than owning to the transition from the impurity level [58]. This indicated that the CQDs were introduced to the BiOI materials in the form of surface modification rather than heteroatom doping. Compared with pure BiOI materials, the CQDs/BiOI materials exhibited enhanced absorption intensity from 520 to 800 nm, and the absorption enhancement was beneficial for forming more electron–hole pairs and improving photocatalytic activity [19]. The band gap energy of the pure BiOI materials was determined to be 1.81 eV through the calculation using the classical Tauc approach (Fig. 5b) [18].

The specific surface area of the semiconductor was considered as important factor that affected the photocatalytic activity. The specific surface area and porosity of the as-prepared materials were investigated by nitrogen adsorption and desorption. As shown in Fig. 6 , the isotherms of the BiOI and CQDs/BiOI samples correspond to type IV with a H3 hysteresis loop, which suggested mesopores features [59]. The BET specific surface area of the 3 wt% CQDs/BiOI, 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI and 10 wt% CQDs/BiOI samples were calculated to be 27.61, 29.22 and 29.24 m2 g−1, which were much larger than the pure BiOI (9.47 m2 g−1). The enhanced specific surface area could enable adsorbing more active species and pollutants on the surface of materials and the CQDs was acted as adsorption centers [60]. In addition, the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method was used to determine the pore volumes of pure BiOI and CQDs/BiOI microsphere samples (inset in Fig. 6). The mesopores in the samples most likely originated from their inter-nanosheet spacing [60]. The result of BET analysis was in accordance with SEM analysis.

To evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the obtained CQDs/BiOI samples, the photocatalytic degradation of RhB was performed under visible light irradiation (Fig. 7 ). For controlling the experiment, no noticeable degradation of RhB was observed without photocatalyst, implying the direct photolysis of RhB can be neglected. After 120 min irradiation, 68.5% of RhB could be degraded by pure BiOI materials. When the CQDs were introduced to the BiOI to construct the CQDs/BiOI hybrid materials, the photocatalytic activity improved significantly. As Fig. 7a shown, the 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI material displayed the highest photocatalytic activity for the RhB degradation and 94.9% of RhB can be degraded after irradiation for 120 min. However, the contents of CQDs have a significant effect on the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/BiOI materials. There have conjugated systems in the CQDs, which enabled the CQDs could acting as electron acceptors to transfer the electrons and thus hindering the electron–hole pairs recombination. When a low amount of CQDs was modified on the BiOI, the CQDs could effectively transfer the photo-electrons from CB of BiOI after being illuminated under visible light while enable the efficient electron–hole pairs separation. As a result, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BiOI materials was improved. However, when the CQDs content was higher than 6 wt%, a further increase of CQDs content caused a decrease in the photocatalytic activity of RhB degradation. This was attributed to the CQDs could absorb visible light. Although the modification of CQDs favored the charge separation, while too many CQDs dispersed on the surface of BiOI would shield the BiOI from absorbing visible light. This could lead to the decrease of light absorption of BiOI with the too much content of CQDs. As is known to all, the semiconductor need to absorb light to generate electron–hole pairs and then take part in the redox reactions. The lower light harvesting could reduce the generation of electron–hole pairs and thus resulted in the decreased photocatalytic activity [61]. The obtained CQDs/BiOI materials also displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity than BiOI microspheres in our previous report [22] and BiOI sphere-like structure in several other literatures [62,63]. The UV–vis absorption spectral changes during the photocatalytic degradation of RhB was shown in Fig. S1. An evident decrease in RhB absorption at λ = 553 nm was observed, accompanied by a shift in the absorption band towards the blue region, which can be ascribed to the step-by-step de-ethylation process [15].

Moreover, it can be found that the photodegradation of RhB followed the pseudo-first-order reaction in this system (Fig. 7b). The rate constants for pure BiOI, 3 wt% CQDs/BiOI, 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI and 10 wt% CQDs/BiOI were 0.0173, 0.0280, 0.0316 and 0.0206 min−1, respectively. The 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI has the maximum rate constant, which was approximate 1.83 times to pure BiOI materials and the result demonstrated that suitable CQDs modification can efficiently improve the photocatalytic activity of BiOI.

The photocurrent tests can be employed to evaluate the ability of generating electron–hole pairs and the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. To better understand the synergic action of CQDs and BiOI materials on enhancing the photocatalytic activity of BiOI, the photocurrent test over the pure BiOI and CQDs/BiOI samples with different CQDs contents were performed. Fig. 8 a showed a plot of transient photocurrent for the pure BiOI and CQDs/BiOI samples under the intermittent irradiation of visible light. The fast transient photocurrent responses for several on–off cycle events have been observed over all the samples, and the CQDs/BiOI materials showed much stronger photocurrent intensities than pure BiOI. With the content of CQDs increased, the photocurrent intensity firstly increased then decreased and the 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI materials displayed the highest photocurrent intensity. This was indicative of the stronger ability to produce charge carriers for CQDs/BiOI materials and the high probability of charge transfer on the interfaces of CQDs and BiOI materials [64].

To further study the interface electron transfer rate of the as-prepared BiOI and CQDs/BiOI materials with different CQDs contents, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed, as shown in Fig. 8b. The EIS analysis displayed that the arc radius on the EIS Nynquist plot of CQDs/BiOI materials was smaller than that of pure BiOI. The 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI materials exhibited the least radius. The smaller of the arc radius in EIS Nynquist plot demonstrated the faster interfacial charge transfer and the higher efficiency of charge separation [65]. This result implied that the photo-generated electrons and holes can be easily separated by the introduction of CQDs.

Photoluminescence (PL) was employed to study the excited state and reveal the differences in the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/BiOI materials due to the PL emission was originated from the recombination of electron–hole pairs [66]. Fig S2 displayed the PL spectra of BiOI and CQDs/BiOI materials with different CQDs contents. The emission peak was centered at around 680 nm under the excitation at 525 nm. The PL emission was originated from the recombination of electron–hole pairs and the emission peak centered at around 680 nm was consistent with the absorption edge in UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra. Clearly, the emission peak around 680 nm had the same variation trend as the photocatalytic activity of different photocatalysts. The 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI materials with the lowest relative PL intensity exhibited the best photocatalytic activity. When the CQDs was introduced, the CQDs/BiOI materials exhibited lower intensity than pure BiOI, which revealing the efficient transfer of photo-generated electrons from BiOI to CQDs and resulting in the effective separation of charge carriers [8,67]. This result was in accordance with the photocurrent and EIS analysis.

The energy levels of valence band maximum (VBM) and conduction band minimum (CBM) played curial roles in determining the redox ability of photogenerated charge carriers in the photo-degradation processes. As an effective photocatalyst, the VB and CB should be located at suitable positions so that the photogenerated charge carriers can take part in decomposing organic molecules. The VB of BiOI primarily derived from the hybridization of the O 2p and I 5p orbitals, and the CB mainly from Bi 6p states [68]. To determine the VB edges of the as-prepared pure BiOI and CQDs/BiOI materials, the total density of VB states were measured by XPS VB spectra (Fig. 9 ) [12,69,70]. The pure BiOI displayed the valence band edge of the maximum energy at about 0.79 eV. Because the band gap of the BiOI is 1.81 eV from the optical absorption spectrum, the CB minimum would occur at about −1.02 eV. For the CQDs/BiOI material, the similar VB edge value can be obtained, which means the introduction of CQDs did not change the electronic density of states of BiOI matrix. The CQDs have not doped into the BiOI but only existed in the form of surface modification for efficient transfer electrons, which was in accordance with the DRS analysis. In addition, it has been widely recognized that the reduction potentials of OH/OH− and O2/O2
− were 2.38 and −0.046 eV vs. NHE, respectively [21,71]. Therefore, it was assumed that the BiOI could not oxidize the surface OH− to generate ·OH, while the BiOI was feasible to trap molecular oxygen to generate O2
−.

To confirm that the O2
− can be generated while the OH cannot, the electron spin resonance (ESR) spin-trap technique was then carried out to investigate the active species generated over BiOI and CQDs/BiOI materials under the visible light irradiation [72,73]. As Fig. 10 a shown, four characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2
− can be observed for the pure BiOI materials under visible light irradiation, which reveals the O2
− can be generated during the photocatalytic process. However, the characteristic signals were weakly, which means the electrons on the CB of BiOI would tend to recombine with the holes on the VB easily and only partly of electrons can trap molecular oxygen to generate O2
−. While the DMPO-OH characteristic peaks can not be detected as it can be inferred that the OH− can not be oxidized to produce OH and OH was not the main reactive species in this system (Fig. 10b). After the CQDs was introduced, the intensity of DMPO-O2
− peaks was much stronger than that of pure BiOI sample (Fig. 10c). This indicated that the CQDs modification lead to efficient separation of electron–hole pairs and more electrons can trap molecular oxygen to generate O2
−. This was ascribed to the delocalized conjugated structure of CQDs enabling the effective transfer of the photo-generated electrons to the CQDs. Similar to BiOI materials, the OH also can not be produced under the visible light irradiation in CQDs/BiOI system (Fig. 10d).

To further reveal the roles of the active species during the degradation process over the CQDs/BiOI materials under visible light irradiation, active species trapping experiments were carried out by adding various trapping agents, such as isopropyl alcohol (IPA) for ·OH, benzoquinone (BQ) for O2
− and ammonium oxalate (AO) for h+ [22,32]. As shown in Fig. 11 , after the addition of IPA, the photocatalytic activity of RhB degradation by 6 wt% CQDs/BiOI materials was not obvious affected. However, obvious inhibition of the photocatalytic performance were observed when BQ or AO was added, confirming the importance role of O2
− and h+ during the photo-degradation process. The results of ESR analysis and active species trapping experiments were in according to the theoretical prediction based on the XPS VB spectra, indicating that the O2
− and h+ were the main active species.

Fig. 12 showed the possible photocatalytic mechanism of the CQDs/BiOI materials. CQDs are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles comprising amorphous to nanocrystalline cores with predominantly graphitic carbon (sp2 carbon) or graphene and graphene oxide sheets fused by diamond-like sp3 hybridized carbon insertions [36,74]. The CQDs possess conjugated systems [37], and that could enable the CQDs acting as electron acceptors to transfer the electrons and thus hindering the recombination of electron–hole pairs. Under visible light irradiation, the electrons can be excited from the VB of BiOI to the CB, leaving the holes on the VB. The photogenerated electrons on the CB of BiOI can effectively reduce O2 to O2
− by one electron process via the CQDs. In this case, the CQDs worked as a cocatalyst for the rapid transfer of photogenerated electrons from the BiOI, resulting in effective separation of electron–hole pairs. From the BET analysis, it was found that the CQDs could act as adsorption centers. It was interesting that the excellent adsorptive performance of CQDs assured the adsorption of pollutant on its surface and the O2
− was formed through O2 capture electron on the CQDs simultaneously. Considering the above results from XPS VB spectra, ESR analysis and active species trapping experiments, the O2
− play importance role during the photo-degradation process, which signifying the CQDs could worked as photocatalytic reaction centers. Therefore, the effective separation and high-efficiency utilization of photogenerated electron–hole pairs could result in the improved photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/BiOI material photocatalysts.

Novel CQDs/BiOI materials have been synthesized via an IL assisted solvothermal method. The one-step formation mechanism of the CQDs/BiOI materials based on the initial formation of strong coupling between the IL and CQDs and subsequent resulted in tight junctions between BiOI and CQDs. Compared with the pure BiOI, the CQDs modified BiOI materials exhibited obviously enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. The improved photocatalytic activity can be assigned to the cooperation effect of the excellent contact interface between BiOI and CQDs, the effective separation of charge carriers via CQDs and the CQDs acted as photocatalytic reaction centers. The O2
− and h+ were the main active species for the photocatalytic degradation. This strategy can be extended to other system by using IL to construct strong-coupling hybrid materials, such as CQDs-semiconductor, graphene-semiconductor, g–C3N4-semiconductor and high-molecular polymer-semiconductor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21206060, 21471069 and 21476098) and the Special Financial Grant from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2013T60506).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.11.015.
